title: ShareArb: Evictor-Side Responsibility for Shared Linux Page-Cache Arbitration
description: ShareArb studies multi-tenant Linux page-cache arbitration when tenants overlap on file-backed pages. It starts from a pCache-style shared/private occupancy baseline and adds one new signal: evictor-side responsibility debt that charges the tenant that displaced a shared page for the predicted downstream harm imposed on the other tenants that were likely to reuse it.
motivation: The broad adjacent space is already occupied: pCache covers shared/private page-cache accounting, Per-VM Page Cache Partitioning covers utility-driven share control with tenant-aware policy choice, and recent systems such as FetchBPF, PageFlex, and cache_ext cover programmable paging mechanisms. The remaining gap is narrower but still important: occupancy accounting says who uses cache space, yet it does not attribute who caused cross-tenant harm when a shared page is evicted under overlap-heavy file-backed workloads.
proposed_approach: ShareArb keeps a conservative pCache-style proportional charging baseline, maintains a Shared Responsibility Vector over the current consumers of each shared page, and charges a decayed debt to the evictor equal to the predicted miss cost imposed on the other tenants. A small controller then uses occupancy plus debt to choose among a few existing replacement profiles and a simplex-style coarse share grid that works for both 2-tenant and 3-tenant cases; when overlap is weak, the debt term is disabled and the method reduces to occupancy-only arbitration.

related_work.title: Precise control of page cache for containers
related_work.how_it_differs: pCache already contributes shared/private page-cache accounting and proportional charging of shared pages. ShareArb assumes that baseline and asks whether arbitration still needs an additional evictor-side responsibility signal.

related_work.title: Per-VM Page Cache Partitioning for Cloud Computing Platforms
related_work.how_it_differs: Per-VM Page Cache Partitioning already studies utility-based share allocation plus tenant-level replacement control. ShareArb uses only a small controller and claims novelty solely in the added responsibility heuristic.

related_work.title: Shared-Page Management for Improving the Temporal Isolation of Memory Reservations in Resource Kernels
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper identifies cross-domain interference from shared-page eviction and proposes protection mechanisms such as Shared-Page Eviction Lock, but it targets real-time memory reservations rather than best-effort Linux page-cache arbitration and does not perform online evictor-side harm attribution.

related_work.title: ROBUS: Fair cache allocation for data-parallel workloads
related_work.how_it_differs: ROBUS studies fairness for shareable cached objects and is conceptually close because one cached object can benefit multiple tenants at once. However, it focuses on batch object allocation in data-parallel systems, not online page eviction in Linux, and it does not charge the evictor for downstream harm after a shared item is displaced.

related_work.title: Towards Application-level I/O Proportionality with a Weight-aware Page Cache Management
related_work.how_it_differs: Justitia shows that page-cache occupancy must sometimes reflect fairness weights, but it does not address overlap-heavy shared-page externalities or evictor-side responsibility.

related_work.title: cache_ext: Customizing the Page Cache with eBPF
related_work.how_it_differs: cache_ext provides mechanism for custom Linux page-cache policies while preserving sharing, but it does not define a multi-tenant arbitration signal for harmful shared-page eviction.

hypothesis: On overlap-heavy file-backed workloads, adding evictor-side responsibility debt on top of pCache-style shared/private occupancy charging will reduce shared-page regret and improve worst-tenant slowdown relative to occupancy-only baselines, while preserving aggregate throughput and reducing to baseline behavior on disjoint workloads.

confirm: Across the overlap-heavy synthetic and SQLite trace-derived families, ShareArb improves worst-tenant slowdown by roughly 8 to 12 percent on average over pCache-Account.
Aggregate throughput proxy remains within 3 percent of pCache-Account in the worst case and usually improves.
Shared-page regret decreases materially relative to both pCache-Account and ShareArb-NoDebt.
Charged debt correlates positively with realized downstream harm on both synthetic and trace-derived workloads.
On the disjoint negative-control family, ShareArb stays within 3 percent of pCache-Account, confirming correct reduction behavior.
Replay-to-live sanity checks preserve the ranking between pCache-Account and ShareArb in at least 3 of 4 tested conditions.
refute: pCache-style shared/private charging alone matches ShareArb on overlap-heavy workloads.
The debt term helps only under one fragile decay or miss-cost setting.
The observed gains disappear on the SQLite trace-derived workloads even if they appear on synthetic traces.
Fairness improvements occur only with severe throughput collapse or unstable share/profile oscillation.
Limited live checks fail to preserve even the direction of replay-predicted improvements.
